questions.question: What is the entanglement entropy of a maximally entangled Bell state |Φ⁺⟩?
questions.explanation: A Bell state has entanglement entropy of 1 ebit. The reduced density matrix is maximally mixed (I/2), giving S(ρA) = 1 bit.

questions.question: What are Schmidt coefficients used for?

options.text: Quantifying entanglement in pure bipartite states

questions.explanation: The Schmidt decomposition |ψ⟩ = Σᵢλᵢ|iA⟩|iB⟩ gives coefficients λᵢ whose entropy measures entanglement in pure bipartite states.

questions.question: What range of values can concurrence take for two qubits?
questions.explanation: Concurrence ranges from 0 (separable states) to 1 (maximally entangled Bell states).

questions.question: What does the negativity measure detect?
questions.explanation: Negativity quantifies the negative eigenvalues of the partial transpose. A negative eigenvalue (NPT) guarantees entanglement.

questions.question: What is bound entanglement?

options.text: Entanglement that cannot be distilled to Bell pairs

questions.explanation: Bound entangled states are entangled but have positive partial transpose (PPT), making it impossible to distill pure entanglement from them.

questions.question: How are GHZ and W states related?

options.text: One can be converted to the other by LOCC
They are SLOCC inequivalent (different entanglement types)
W is GHZ with an extra qubit

questions.explanation: GHZ and W states represent fundamentally different types of tripartite entanglement and cannot be converted into each other even probabilistically by LOCC.

questions.question: Entanglement can increase under local operations and classical communication (LOCC).
questions.explanation: False. A fundamental requirement for any valid entanglement measure is that it cannot increase under LOCC. Entanglement is a nonlocal resource.
